Case Study · UX Research & Design Strategy

From Supply Chain Data to Client Value Stories

Turning a fragmented, siloed reporting process into a shared, buildable direction for a client-facing value platform.

Scott Faranello · Lead UX Researcher & Design Strategist

Overview

At a Glance

Role
Lead UX Researcher & Design Strategist
Context
A client-facing value-reporting platform for quarterly business reviews, populated by data pulled from multiple siloed internal systems.
Methods
Discovery research, qualitative interviews, thematic synthesis, end-to-end journey mapping, information architecture, wireframing, prototype testing, design iteration.
Deliverables
Research findings, user journey, jobs-to-be-done, use cases, information architecture, wireframe designs, and annotated design and interaction instructions for the development team.
Scope
Discovery research, experience strategy, information architecture, interaction design, user validation, mid-fidelity wireframes.

01 · The Situation

The data to tell a value story — and no way to tell it

A large, regulated healthcare distributor had the data to tell clients a value story, but no way to do so quickly, consistently, and easily.

Account reps had strong client relationships and met quarterly for business reviews. Leadership wanted more from those conversations: the value clients received beyond the cost of goods — the supply-chain efficiencies and operational savings clients were getting but couldn't quantify on their own.

To tell that story, account teams needed data and analysis. The data existed — but it lived in disconnected, siloed systems owned by teams who weren't always willing to share it.

In its current state, preparing for a quarterly review meant days of manual effort just to identify and track down the data, and understand it well enough to walk into a client conversation with a compelling story.

Stakeholder Challenges

  • Highly regulated environment
  • Multiple system owners
  • Siloed data sources
  • Convoluted data
  • Value stories are hard to tell

02 · The Main Problem

Nobody could see the whole picture at once

Initial conversations with stakeholders showed that nobody — not the stakeholders, not the data team, not the account teams, not the vendor building the infrastructure — had a complete understanding of what needed to be built or how it would actually work.

Each team owned a piece of that knowledge, but none could see it in full.

That was the gap I was there to close.

Product Challenges

  • Data continuity — the story had to hold together across systems that didn't talk to each other.
  • Ease of use — prep had to move from days of manual effort to something a rep could trust and reuse.
  • Narrative support — the tool had to help reps tell a value story, not just display numbers.
  • Client-centricity — everything had to ladder back to what the client actually valued.

03 · My Process

Start with business outcome, not an interface

Every interview I had with account reps uncovered another piece of the puzzle — but no one could explain the entire process end-to-end, because no one actually owned it.

The design challenge wasn't about creating a dashboard. It was about understanding what they needed to build, and answering the questions that would tell us whether it was worth building at all:

  1. Do account reps understand the value they deliver beyond cost?
  2. Would account reps actually use a tool like this?
  3. Would data pulled from different systems tell a compelling value story?
  4. How would stakeholders define success?
  5. What did account reps need to accomplish in client conversations?

Research Activities

  • Recruited 12+ account representatives across a mix of territories, experience levels, and client portfolios.
  • Conducted semi-structured 1:1 interviews focused on preparation workflows.
  • Asked probing questions: How do you prepare for a quarterly review? Where does each metric come from? Do you understand the data? What's missing? How confident are you in it?
  • Used affinity mapping to identify recurring themes.
  • Organized the data pipelines into value buckets that drove the information architecture.

04 · Discovery

The effort wasn't the problem. The system was.

The problem wasn't that reps lacked effort. It was that the current system made a value story difficult to tell.

Preparing for a single client review often meant opening multiple disconnected systems, exporting spreadsheets, requesting reports from other departments, and manually combining everything into PowerPoint. Much of that work wasn't analysis — it was simply hunting for information.

Even after spending days assembling the presentation, reps still questioned whether the numbers were current or complete. Because each report came from a different source, they spent valuable meeting time explaining where the data came from — instead of discussing the value they'd delivered to the client.

05 · From Insight to Design

Making the invisible visible — and buildable

From the themes, I mapped the end-to-end journey, defined the information architecture, and built wireframes that made the intended experience visible and discussable across the whole team.

The wireframes were the turning point. The vendor had struggled to understand what they were building. Once they could see it, the work moved. Three rounds of prototype testing followed, iterating against real user needs and what was feasibly buildable.

During testing, several participants naturally looked for a client-level summary before diving into detailed metrics. Our original navigation surfaced operational data first, forcing users to piece the story together themselves. We reorganized the experience around the client narrative — starting with overall value delivered, then letting users drill into supporting metrics.

Catching that before development avoided building the information architecture around the internal data model instead of the user's mental model — which would have required significant rework later.

Deliverables Produced

  • Service context & actors
  • End-to-end user journey
  • Information architecture
  • Validated wireframe framework

06 · Delivery Decisions & Outcomes

Configure what exists, don't build from scratch

Stakeholders chose to deliver through an existing platform with an embedded analytics dashboard rather than build from scratch. My validated design gave them a predefined framework to build against.

As discovery progressed, it became clear that the core challenge wasn't a lack of reporting technology — it was the absence of a shared workflow and information structure. Stakeholders, including business leadership, the data team, and the implementation vendor, weighed the cost of building a new application against extending an existing analytics platform.

My research and validated wireframes demonstrated that the experience could be delivered by configuring the existing platform around the user workflow, rather than creating an entirely new product. That decision reduced implementation risk while preserving the user experience established through research.

Outcomes

  • Three rounds of testing before engineering resolved design questions early and prevented costly downstream rework.
  • The research-defined information architecture and narrative flows held intact from discovery through to the delivered build.
  • Wireframes created the shared understanding that unblocked a stalled vendor team.
  • The design reframed the account conversation from features and pricing toward client value — the outcome leadership asked for.
  • Days of manual preparation per review were designed out, replaced by a single, consistent source of truth.

07 · Reflection

Complex problems don't need more process. They need clarity.

I would push earlier to get the data team and the vendor into the same room as the account reps, instead of bridging between them through my own synthesis. The shared picture I built through research was the thing that unblocked the work. Creating it sooner, together, would have saved weeks.

On a complex system, the fastest path is often getting everyone to see the same thing at the same time. By grounding the work in real user conversations, using design to create visibility and collaboration, and keeping the business goal in front of every decision, I helped a cross-functional team move from ambiguity to a validated, buildable direction.

That's Business-First UX.

Not a report and not a recommendation, but a shared understanding that makes the right thing possible — before it's too late to change course.

Let's connect if you:

  • Are moving fast and making product decisions without enough confidence in them.
  • Have research needs and no senior person to own them right now.
  • Have invested in UX before and struggled to point to what it actually changed.
  • Need someone who can step in, ramp up fast, and deliver without hand-holding.
